title: Comparison between virtual audits and Gemini
text: To compare built environment characteristics collected through virtual audit and automated assessment using Gemini in a subsample of street segments from Belo Horizonte.

title: Comparison between virtual audits and Gemini
text: To compare built environment characteristics collected through virtual audit and automated assessment using Gemini in a subsample of street segments from Belo Horizonte.

title: Comparison between virtual audits and Gemini
text:
2 Virtual audit
The ELSI-Urbe Virtual Audit subproject was conducted using a systematic observation method between April and October 2024, based on available Google Street View images. The audit was performed at the street-segment level, considering both sides of the street, and later aggregated into census tracts, which were defined as neighborhoods. The sampling process was carried out in stages, sequentially selecting municipalities (the most populous municipality participating in ELSI-Brazil from each federative unit, with 20 available), census tracts (with ELSI-Brazil interviews), and random street segments. Data collection was conducted by a single trained rater using a multidimensional instrument and a field manual.
In the present analysis, we included only street segments from Belo Horizonte with at least five audited built environment characteristics available (n = 93 segments), namely: sidewalks, crosswalks, speed bumps, potholes, traffic lights, stop signs, sidewalk obstructions, bike lanes, trees, medians, lane markings, crossing signs, bus lanes, bus stops, and roundabouts.

title: Comparison between virtual audits and Gemini
text:
3 Gemini
The same street segments and image dates included in the virtual audit were automatically processed using Google Street View images and a multimodal large language model (Gemini, Google) through a fully automated pipeline. For each coordinate, eight static images covering the full 360° view were selected within a 10-meter radius. These images were downloaded, preprocessed, and analyzed.
A prompt-based approach was used to classify built environment characteristics under two classification schemes (binary and categorical) using four multimodal large language models: Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Gemini 3 Flash, and Gemini 3.1 Flash.
In addition to the characteristics comparable to the virtual audit, the automated approach also evaluated a broader set of characteristics, including: curbs, street lights, parked vehicles, yield signs, pedestrian signals, kiosks, bollards, median barriers, traffic signs, school zones, parking lanes, and BRT stations.
However, for the present analyses, only characteristics comparable to the virtual audit were considered, resulting in a final sample of 60 street segments with complete data from both approaches.

title: Comparison between virtual audits and Gemini
text:
4 Statistical analysis
Descriptive frequencies were calculated for each characteristic in both approaches. Agreement between the virtual audit and the automated assessment was evaluated using observed agreement (%) and Cohen’s kappa coefficient, with corresponding 95% confidence intervals.
Kappa values were not estimated for characteristics with insufficient variability (i.e., when one or both approaches classified all observations into a single category), and were therefore reported as not applicable (NA).
The automated assessment using Gemini was implemented in Python. Data management and dataset organization were conducted in Stata, and all statistical analyses were performed in R.


Packages
rm(list = ls())

library(pacman)
p_load(
  # Data manipulation and wrangling
  tidyverse,
  dplyr,
  purrr,
  here,
  # Data import
  haven,
  readxl,
  # Descriptive statistics
  janitor,
  gtsummary,
  # Agreement / reliability
  irr,
  psych,
  # Diagnostic performance / ROC
  pROC,
  # Tables and reporting
  gt,
  kableExtra,
  patchwork,
  # Label repulsion
  ggrepel
)




Load processed prediction datasets
prediction_datasets <- list(
  legacy_2_5_flash = readRDS(here(
    "predictions",
    "streetview_predictions_dates_legacy_2.5-flash.rds"
  )),
  legacy_2_5_pro = readRDS(here(
    "predictions",
    "streetview_predictions_dates_legacy_2.5-pro.rds"
  )),
  #  legacy_3_flash = readRDS(here(
  #    "predictions",
  #    "streetview_predictions_dates_legacy_3-flash.rds"
  #  )),
  legacy_3_1_flash = readRDS(here(
    "predictions",
    "streetview_predictions_dates_legacy_3.1-flash.rds"
  ))
)

title: Comparison between virtual audits and Gemini
text:
5 Results
Agreement between the audit and the Gemini models ranged from slight to substantial. Gemini 2.5 Flash generally outperformed Gemini 2.5 Pro, yielding higher kappa values for most variables with estimable agreement. Substantial agreement was observed for median, traffic light, and bus stop in both models, although kappa values were consistently higher for Gemini 2.5 Flash. Moderate agreement was found for poor street condition (pothole), while fair agreement was observed for crosswalk, speed bumps, stop sign, lane markings, pedestrian crossing sign, and roundabout. Sidewalk obstruction showed slight agreement in Gemini 2.5 Flash and non-estimable kappa in Gemini 2.5 Pro.
Kappa could not be estimated for sidewalk, bike lane, bus lane, and trees because of insufficient variability in ratings. Although observed agreement was generally high (>80% for most variables), kappa values were substantially lower for several characteristics, indicating that part of the agreement was attributable to chance and to the highly imbalanced prevalence of some features.

5.1 Descriptive


Descriptive table function
# Define variables
vars_base <- c(
  "swalk_pres_cat",
  "str_cwalk",
  "str_scont1",
  "str_cond_cat2",
  "str_tcont3",
  "str_tcont1",
  "swalk_cont_cat2",
  "str_blane",
  "veg_tree_cat2",
  "str_med_cat2",
  "str_lanevis_cat2",
  "str_cstop_cat2",
  "trans_blane",
  "trans_stop",
  "str_tcont2"
)

# Define variable labels
var_labels <- c(
  swalk_pres_cat = "Sidewalk",
  str_cwalk = "Crosswalk",
  str_scont1 = "Speed bumps",
  str_cond_cat2 = "Poor street condition (pothole)",
  str_tcont3 = "Traffic light",
  str_tcont1 = "Stop sign",
  swalk_cont_cat2 = "Sidewalk obstruction",
  str_blane = "Bike lane",
  veg_tree_cat2 = "Trees",
  str_med_cat2 = "Median",
  str_lanevis_cat2 = "Lane markings",
  str_cstop_cat2 = "Pedestrian crossing sign",
  trans_blane = "Bus lane",
  trans_stop = "Bus stop",
  str_tcont2 = "Roundabout"
)

# Calculate prevalence
calc_prop <- function(data, var, suffix) {
  mean(data[[paste0(var, suffix)]], na.rm = TRUE)
}

# Calculate observed agreement
calc_agreement <- function(data, var) {
  x <- data[[paste0(var, "_0")]]
  y <- data[[paste0(var, "_1")]]
  mean(x == y, na.rm = TRUE)
}

# Calculate Cohen's kappa and confidence intervals
calc_kappa <- function(data, var) {
  x <- data[[paste0(var, "_0")]]
  y <- data[[paste0(var, "_1")]]
  valid <- !is.na(x) & !is.na(y)
  x <- x[valid]
  y <- y[valid]
  if (length(unique(x)) < 2 | length(unique(y)) < 2) {
    return(c(
      kappa = NA,
      lwr = NA,
      upr = NA
    ))
  }
  k <- psych::cohen.kappa(cbind(x, y))
  ci <- k$confid[1, ]
  c(
    kappa = unname(ci["estimate"]),
    lwr = unname(ci["lower"]),
    upr = unname(ci["upper"])
  )
}

# Build descriptive agreement table
build_table <- function(data) {
  purrr::map_dfr(vars_base, function(v) {
    k <- calc_kappa(data, v)
    tibble::tibble(
      variable = var_labels[v],
      audit = calc_prop(data, v, "_0"),
      gemini = calc_prop(data, v, "_1"),
      agreement = calc_agreement(data, v),
      kappa = k["kappa"],
      kappa_lwr = k["lwr"],
      kappa_upr = k["upr"]
    )
  }) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      audit = audit * 100,
      gemini = gemini * 100,
      agreement = agreement * 100
    )
}

# Create descriptive tables for all models
tables <- purrr::map(
  prediction_datasets,
  build_table
)

# Format descriptive agreement tables
format_gt_table <- function(data, title) {
  data %>%
    gt() %>%
    fmt_number(
      columns = c(audit, gemini, agreement),
      decimals = 1
    ) %>%
    fmt_number(
      columns = c(kappa, kappa_lwr, kappa_upr),
      decimals = 2
    ) %>%
    cols_label(
      variable = "Variable",
      audit = "Audit (%)",
      gemini = "Gemini (%)",
      agreement = "Observed agreement (%)",
      kappa = "Kappa",
      kappa_lwr = "Lower CI",
      kappa_upr = "Upper CI"
    ) %>%
    tab_header(
      title = title
    ) %>%
    tab_source_note(
      source_note = paste(
        "Kappa was not estimated (NA) for variables with",
        "insufficient variability (i.e., when one or both",
        "raters had only one category)."
      )
    )
}

# Create formatted tables for all models
gt_tables <- list(
  "Gemini 2.5 Flash" = format_gt_table(
    tables$legacy_2_5_flash,
    "Gemini 2.5 Flash"
  ),
  "Gemini 2.5 Pro" = format_gt_table(
    tables$legacy_2_5_pro,
    "Gemini 2.5 Pro"
  ),
  #  "Gemini 3 Flash" =
  #    format_gt_table(
  #      tables$legacy_3_flash,
  #      "Gemini 3 Flash"
  #    ),
  "Gemini 3.1 Flash" = format_gt_table(
    tables$legacy_3_1_flash,
    "Gemini 3.1 Flash"
  )
)

5.2 Kappa


Kappa plot
# Combine descriptive tables from all models
plot_data <- purrr::imap_dfr(
  tables,
  ~ dplyr::mutate(.x, model = .y)
) %>%
  mutate(
    model = recode(
      model,
      legacy_2_5_flash = "Gemini 2.5 Flash",
      legacy_2_5_pro = "Gemini 2.5 Pro",
      legacy_3_flash = "Gemini 3 Flash",
      legacy_3_1_flash = "Gemini 3.1 Flash"
    ),
    kappa_plot = ifelse(is.na(kappa), -0.35, kappa),
    lk_cat = case_when(
      is.na(kappa) ~ "Not estimable",
      kappa < 0 ~ "Poor",
      kappa < 0.21 ~ "Slight",
      kappa < 0.41 ~ "Fair",
      kappa < 0.61 ~ "Moderate",
      kappa < 0.81 ~ "Substantial",
      TRUE ~ "Almost perfect"
    ),
    lk_cat = factor(
      lk_cat,
      levels = c(
        "Almost perfect",
        "Substantial",
        "Moderate",
        "Fair",
        "Slight",
        "Poor",
        "Not estimable"
      )
    )
  )
# Order variables by mean kappa across models
var_order <- plot_data %>%
  group_by(variable) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_kappa = mean(kappa, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(mean_kappa) %>%
  pull(variable)
# Create vertical offsets for each model
offsets <- c(
  "Gemini 2.5 Flash" = -0.30,
  "Gemini 2.5 Pro" = -0.10,
  "Gemini 3 Flash" = 0.10,
  "Gemini 3.1 Flash" = 0.30
)
# Prepare plotting coordinates
plot_data <- plot_data %>%
  mutate(
    variable = factor(variable, levels = var_order),
    y = as.numeric(variable) + offsets[model]
  )
# Create kappa comparison plot
ggplot(plot_data) +
  geom_errorbarh(
    data = filter(plot_data, !is.na(kappa)),
    aes(
      y = y,
      xmin = kappa_lwr,
      xmax = kappa_upr,
      color = lk_cat
    ),
    height = 0,
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(
      x = kappa_plot,
      y = y,
      color = lk_cat,
      shape = model
    ),
    size = 4
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq_along(var_order),
    labels = var_order
  ) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(
      "Gemini 2.5 Flash" = 16,
      "Gemini 2.5 Pro" = 17,
      "Gemini 3 Flash" = 15,
      "Gemini 3.1 Flash" = 18
    )
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Almost perfect" = "#1B9E77",
      "Substantial" = "#1F78B4",
      "Moderate" = "#A6CEE3",
      "Fair" = "#FFD92F",
      "Slight" = "#FC8D62",
      "Poor" = "#D73027",
      "Not estimable" = "#7F7F7F"
    ),
    breaks = c(
      "Almost perfect",
      "Substantial",
      "Moderate",
      "Fair",
      "Slight",
      "Poor",
      "Not estimable"
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(-0.45, 1),
    breaks = seq(-0.4, 1, by = 0.2)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "\nCohen's kappa (95% CI)",
    y = "",
    color = "Agreement level",
    shape = "Model"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "right"
  )











Kappa and agreement plot
# Create agreement versus kappa plot
ggplot(
  plot_data,
  aes(
    x = agreement,
    y = kappa_plot
  )
) +
  geom_point(
    aes(color = lk_cat),
    size = 4
  ) +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = variable),
    size = 3.8,
    box.padding = 0.25,
    point.padding = 0.15,
    force = 1,
    max.overlaps = Inf,
    segment.alpha = 0.4,
    min.segment.length = 0
  ) +
  facet_wrap(
    ~model,
    ncol = 2
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Almost perfect" = "#1B9E77",
      "Substantial" = "#1F78B4",
      "Moderate" = "#A6CEE3",
      "Fair" = "#FFD92F",
      "Slight" = "#FC8D62",
      "Poor" = "#D73027",
      "Not estimable" = "#7F7F7F"
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.08))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.10))
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "\nObserved agreement (%)",
    y = "Cohen's kappa\n",
    color = "Agreement level"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 15) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.spacing = unit(1, "cm"),
    plot.margin = margin(5, 20, 5, 5),
    strip.text = element_text(
      size = 14,
      face = "bold"
    )
  )











Friedman test data preparation
# Create a long dataset with kappa values
kappa_long <- purrr::imap_dfr(
  tables,
  ~ dplyr::transmute(
    .x,
    variable,
    kappa,
    model = .y
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    model = recode(
      model,
      legacy_2_5_flash = "Gemini 2.5 Flash",
      legacy_2_5_pro = "Gemini 2.5 Pro",
      legacy_3_flash = "Gemini 3 Flash",
      legacy_3_1_flash = "Gemini 3.1 Flash"
    )
  )
# Keep variables with at least one estimable kappa
kappa_long_analysis <- kappa_long %>%
  group_by(variable) %>%
  filter(any(!is.na(kappa))) %>%
  ungroup()
# Create a complete dataset for the Friedman test
kappa_wide_complete <- kappa_long %>%
  select(
    variable,
    model,
    kappa
  ) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = model,
    values_from = kappa
  ) %>%
  tidyr::drop_na()
# Perform the Friedman test
friedman_result <- friedman.test(
  as.matrix(
    kappa_wide_complete[, -1]
  )
)
# Perform pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank tests
pairwise_results <- pairwise.wilcox.test(
  x = kappa_long_analysis$kappa,
  g = kappa_long_analysis$model,
  paired = FALSE,
  p.adjust.method = "holm"
)
# Summarize kappa performance by model
kappa_summary <- kappa_long_analysis %>%
  group_by(model) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_kappa = mean(kappa, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_kappa = median(kappa, na.rm = TRUE),
    min_kappa = min(kappa, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_kappa = max(kappa, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_variables = sum(!is.na(kappa)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(
    desc(mean_kappa)
  )
# Count the number of variables won by each model
model_wins <- kappa_long_analysis %>%
  filter(!is.na(kappa)) %>%
  group_by(variable) %>%
  slice_max(
    kappa,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) %>%
  count(
    model,
    name = "wins"
  ) %>%
  arrange(
    desc(wins)
  )
# Create a table of variables included in the Friedman test
friedman_variables <- kappa_wide_complete %>%
  select(variable) %>%
  arrange(variable)




Kappa comparison table
# Create a wide kappa comparison table
kappa_table <- kappa_long_analysis %>%
  select(
    variable,
    model,
    kappa
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = model,
    values_from = kappa
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    mean_kappa = rowMeans(
      select(., -variable),
      na.rm = TRUE
    )
  ) %>%
  arrange(mean_kappa) %>%
  select(-mean_kappa)
# Calculate mean kappa for each model
mean_row <- kappa_table %>%
  summarise(
    across(
      -variable,
      ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
    )
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    variable = "Mean",
    .before = 1
  )
# Calculate wins by model
winner_vector <- apply(
  kappa_table[, -1],
  1,
  function(x) {

    x[is.na(x)] <- -Inf

    names(x)[which.max(x)]

  }
)
wins <- tibble(
  winner = winner_vector
) %>%
  count(
    winner,
    name = "wins"
  )
# Create wins row
wins_row <- tibble(
  variable = "Wins"
)
for (model_name in names(kappa_table)[-1]) {

  value <- wins %>%
    filter(
      winner == model_name
    ) %>%
    pull(wins)

  wins_row[[model_name]] <- ifelse(
    length(value) == 0,
    0,
    value
  )

}
# Append summary rows
kappa_table <- bind_rows(
  kappa_table,
  mean_row,
  wins_row
)
# Display table
kappa_table %>%
  gt() %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = -variable,
    rows = variable != "Wins",
    decimals = 3
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = -variable,
    rows = variable == "Wins",
    decimals = 0
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = variable %in% c(
        "Mean",
        "Wins"
      )
    )
  ) %>%
  cols_label(
    variable = "Variable"
  ) %>%
  tab_header(
    title = "Cohen's Kappa by Variable and Model"
  )

5.3 Diagnostic performance
Sensitivity and specificity, which reflect the ability to correctly identify the presence or absence of a characteristic using the field audit as the reference, were generally high for more visually distinctive features, including trees, sidewalks, traffic lights, roundabouts, medians, and bus stops. Both Gemini models achieved perfect or near-perfect sensitivity for sidewalks, trees, medians, and roundabouts, while specificity was particularly high for trees, traffic lights, speed bumps, and roundabouts. In contrast, lower sensitivity and/or specificity values were observed for speed bumps, stop signs, crosswalks, lane markings, and poor street condition (pothole), indicating greater difficulty in detecting or distinguishing these features consistently.
Positive predictive value (PPV), which indicates the probability that a feature is truly present when classified as present, was highest for trees and sidewalks (PPV = 1.00), followed by lane markings, potholes, and bus stops. Lower PPV values were observed for roundabouts, crosswalks, pedestrian crossing signs, and traffic lights, suggesting a higher proportion of false-positive classifications for these characteristics. Negative predictive value (NPV), which indicates the probability that a feature is truly absent when classified as absent, was generally high across most variables, reaching 1.00 for trees, sidewalks, medians, and roundabouts. However, NPV was substantially lower for lane markings, stop signs, potholes, and sidewalk obstructions, particularly for Gemini 2.5 Pro.
Similarly, F1-scores, which summarize the balance between sensitivity and PPV, were highest for trees and sidewalks (F1 = 1.00), followed by lane markings, potholes, medians, bus stops, traffic lights, and sidewalk obstructions. Lower F1-scores were observed for crosswalks, pedestrian crossing signs, stop signs, speed bumps, and roundabouts, reflecting the challenges in achieving both high detection rates and high classification precision for these features. Overall, performance was broadly similar between Gemini 2.5 Flash and Gemini 2.5 Pro, with Gemini 2.5 Flash showing slightly higher F1-scores for several variables, including medians, traffic lights, lane markings, bus stops, and stop signs.


Diagnostic performance data preparation
# Calculate diagnostic performance metrics
calc_metrics <- function(data, var) {
  ref <- data[[paste0(var, "_0")]]
  test <- data[[paste0(var, "_1")]]
  valid <- !is.na(ref) & !is.na(test)
  ref <- ref[valid]
  test <- test[valid]
  TP <- sum(ref == 1 & test == 1)
  TN <- sum(ref == 0 & test == 0)
  FP <- sum(ref == 0 & test == 1)
  FN <- sum(ref == 1 & test == 0)
  tibble(
    variable = var_labels[var],
    sensitivity = ifelse(
      (TP + FN) == 0,
      NA,
      TP / (TP + FN)
    ),
    specificity = ifelse(
      (TN + FP) == 0,
      NA,
      TN / (TN + FP)
    ),
    ppv = ifelse(
      (TP + FP) == 0,
      NA,
      TP / (TP + FP)
    ),
    npv = ifelse(
      (TN + FN) == 0,
      NA,
      TN / (TN + FN)
    ),
    f1_score = ifelse(
      (2 * TP + FP + FN) == 0,
      NA,
      2 * TP / (2 * TP + FP + FN)
    )
  )
}
# Calculate diagnostic performance metrics for all models
metrics_long <- purrr::imap_dfr(
  prediction_datasets,
  function(data, model_name) {
    purrr::map_dfr(
      vars_base,
      ~ calc_metrics(data, .x)
    ) %>%
      mutate(
        model = recode(
          model_name,
          legacy_2_5_flash = "Gemini 2.5 Flash",
          legacy_2_5_pro = "Gemini 2.5 Pro",
          legacy_3_flash = "Gemini 3 Flash",
          legacy_3_1_flash = "Gemini 3.1 Flash"
        )
      )
  }
) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(
      sensitivity,
      specificity,
      ppv,
      npv,
      f1_score
    ),
    names_to = "metric",
    values_to = "value"
  )
# Create diagnostic performance plotting function
plot_metric <- function(metric_name, title_text) {
  ggplot(
    filter(metrics_long, metric == metric_name),
    aes(
      x = variable,
      y = value,
      fill = model
    )
  ) +
    geom_col(
      position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
      width = 0.7
    ) +
    scale_fill_manual(
      values = c(
        "Gemini 2.5 Flash" = "#1B9E77",
        "Gemini 2.5 Pro" = "#1F78B4",
        "Gemini 3 Flash" = "#A6CEE3",
        "Gemini 3.1 Flash" = "#6A3D9A"
      )
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(
      limits = c(0, 1),
      breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)
    ) +
    labs(
      title = title_text,
      x = NULL,
      y = "Value\n",
      fill = NULL
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
    theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      axis.text.x = element_text(
        angle = 45,
        hjust = 1,
        vjust = 1
      )
    )
}




Sensitivity plot
plot_metric(
  "sensitivity",
  "Sensitivity"
)











Specificity plot
plot_metric(
  "specificity",
  "Specificity"
)











PPV plot
plot_metric(
  "ppv",
  "Positive Predictive Value (PPV)"
)











NPV plot
plot_metric(
  "npv",
  "Negative Predictive Value (NPV)"
)











F1-score plot
plot_metric(
  "f1_score",
  "F1-score"
)











5.4 ROC
Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analyses, which evaluate discriminatory ability by comparing sensitivity with the false positive rate, generally supported the findings from the agreement and classification metrics. The highest discriminatory performance was observed for roundabouts, medians, traffic lights, bus stops, and pedestrian crossing signs. Crosswalks, lane markings, potholes, and stop signs showed moderate discrimination, while speed bumps and particularly sidewalk obstruction exhibited the weakest performance. Differences between Gemini 2.5 Flash and Gemini 2.5 Pro were generally small; Gemini 2.5 Flash showed slightly better discrimination for crosswalks, lane markings, stop signs, and potholes, whereas Gemini 2.5 Pro performed marginally better for speed bumps.


ROC panel
# Calculate ROC curve data
calc_roc_data <- function(data, var, model_name) {
  ref <- data[[paste0(var, "_0")]]
  pred <- data[[paste0(var, "_1")]]
  valid <- !is.na(ref) & !is.na(pred)
  ref <- ref[valid]
  pred <- pred[valid]
  if (length(unique(ref)) < 2) {
    return(NULL)
  }
  roc_obj <- pROC::roc(
    ref,
    pred,
    quiet = TRUE
  )
  tibble(
    specificity = roc_obj$specificities,
    sensitivity = roc_obj$sensitivities
  ) %>%
    mutate(
      fpr = 1 - specificity,
      variable = var_labels[var],
      auc = as.numeric(
        pROC::auc(roc_obj)
      ),
      model = model_name
    )
}
# Calculate ROC curves for all models
roc_panel <- purrr::imap_dfr(
  prediction_datasets,
  function(data, model_name) {
    model_label <- dplyr::recode(
      model_name,
      legacy_2_5_flash = "Gemini 2.5 Flash",
      legacy_2_5_pro = "Gemini 2.5 Pro",
      legacy_3_flash = "Gemini 3 Flash",
      legacy_3_1_flash = "Gemini 3.1 Flash"
    )
    purrr::map_dfr(
      vars_base,
      ~ calc_roc_data(
        data,
        .x,
        model_label
      )
    )
  }
)
# Create ROC panel plot
ggplot(
  roc_panel,
  aes(
    x = fpr,
    y = sensitivity,
    color = model
  )
) +
  geom_line(
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_abline(
    slope = 1,
    intercept = 0,
    linetype = 2,
    color = "gray60"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(
    ~variable,
    ncol = 4
  ) +
  coord_equal() +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "Gemini 2.5 Flash" = "#1f78b4",
      "Gemini 2.5 Pro" = "#33a02c",
      "Gemini 3 Flash" = "#ff7f00",
      "Gemini 3.1 Flash" = "#6a3d9a"
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 1),
    breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.5)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 1),
    breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.5)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "\nFalse Positive Rate",
    y = "True Positive Rate\n",
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )









Overall, across agreement, classification, and discrimination metrics, Gemini 2.5 Flash generally performed slightly better than Gemini 2.5 Pro, although differences were modest. Both models achieved their best results for larger, permanent, and visually distinctive urban features, such as bus stops, medians, traffic lights, trees, and roundabouts. Performance was more variable for crosswalks, pedestrian crossing signs, stop signs, lane markings, potholes, and speed bumps, while sidewalk obstruction consistently showed the weakest results. These findings suggest that multimodal artificial intelligence may be a useful tool for automated urban audits of clearly visible built-environment characteristics, although caution is warranted when evaluating more ambiguous, context-dependent, or less visually salient street features.

title: Comparison between virtual audits and Gemini
text:
6 Discrepancy analysis
In this step, discrepancies between human and AI assessments are examined for features selected due to their intermediate and lower performance in agreement and diagnostic metrics. For each variable, segments with disagreement are identified, the type of discrepancy is classified (human = 1 and AI = 0; or human = 0 and AI = 1), and these cases are linked to geographic coordinates and Google Street View links, enabling visual inspection and interpretation of the differences.


Extract identifiers and create Street View tables with discrepancies
# Select Gemini 3.1 Flash dataset
df_model <- prediction_datasets$legacy_3_1_flash %>%
  mutate(
    identificador = as.character(identificador)
  )
# Function to identify disagreement cases
get_ids_discrepancy <- function(data, base_var) {
  var0 <- paste0(base_var, "_0") # Human audit
  var1 <- paste0(base_var, "_1") # Gemini prediction
  data %>%
    dplyr::filter(
      !is.na(.data[[var0]]),
      !is.na(.data[[var1]]),
      .data[[var0]] != .data[[var1]]
    ) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      discrepancy_type = dplyr::case_when(
        .data[[var0]] == 1 &
          .data[[var1]] == 0 ~ "human_1_ai_0",
        .data[[var0]] == 0 &
          .data[[var1]] == 1 ~ "human_0_ai_1"
      )
    ) %>%
    dplyr::select(
      identificador,
      discrepancy_type
    ) %>%
    distinct()
}
# Variables selected for qualitative review
vars_to_use <- c(
  "swalk_cont_cat2",
  "str_lanevis_cat2",
  "str_cond_cat2",
  "str_scont1",
  "str_tcont1",
  "str_cwalk"
)
# Extract discrepant identifiers
ids_list <- lapply(
  vars_to_use,
  function(v) {
    get_ids_discrepancy(
      df_model,
      v
    )
  }
)
names(ids_list) <- var_labels[vars_to_use]
# Load coordinates
coords <- read_excel(
  "virtual_audit/items_coordinates_belo_horizonte 5more.xlsx"
) %>%
  mutate(
    identificador = as.character(id),
    lat = as.numeric(lat),
    lon = as.numeric(lon)
  )
# Create Street View review tables
views <- list()
for (var_name in names(ids_list)) {
  df_view <- coords %>%
    dplyr::inner_join(
      ids_list[[var_name]],
      by = "identificador"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      lat_lon = paste0(
        lat,
        ", ",
        lon
      ),
      streetview_url = paste0(
        "https://www.google.com/maps/@?api=1&map_action=pano&viewpoint=",
        lat,
        ",",
        lon
      )
    ) %>%
    dplyr::select(
      identificador,
      discrepancy_type,
      lat_lon,
      streetview_url
    ) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(
      discrepancy_type,
      as.numeric(identificador)
    )
  views[[var_name]] <- df_view
}



6.1 Sidewalk obstructions
Items marked as present by the AI but absent by human raters are likely due to obstructions that still allow pedestrian passage along the sidewalk. Conversely, cases marked as absent by the AI but present by human raters were often due to obstructions caused by trees or vegetation.
 human_0_ai_1 human_1_ai_0


6.2 Lane markings
Are items marked as present by the AI but absent by human raters due to crosswalk markings or differences in pavement color? Conversely, cases marked as absent by the AI but present by human raters were likely due to the markings being clearly visible in the images.
 human_0_ai_1 human_1_ai_0


6.3 Poor street condition (potholes)
We assessed “What is the apparent condition of the roadway?” and, for this analysis, categorized it as 1 (“Bad,” “Poor,” and “Fair”) and 0 (“Good” and “Very good”). This classification approach may influence discrepancies in responses. In addition, perceptions of roadway quality based on lived experience may differ from how the AI identifies potholes.
 human_0_ai_1 human_1_ai_0


6.4 Speed bumps
The discrepancies observed were only cases in which the human rater identified the feature while the AI did not, likely due to differences in speed bump structures in Brazil.
 human_1_ai_0 human_1_ai_0


6.5 Stop signs
“CANVAS Instructions: Indicate the presence of a device to direct or control traffic with regard to the intersection closest to the green marker. The traffic control device may be located outside the segment as long as it controls the green marker intersection—for example, a stop sign that controls entry into a one-way segment may be on the opposite side of the intersection as the segment but would still be indicated by this item.”
The observed discrepancies are likely due to differences in how the measure was applied, as we considered the stop sign only at the starting point of the segment.


6.6 Crosswalks
“CANVAS Instructions: Indicate the presence of a crosswalk in the segment. Consider only crosswalks that are at the intersection closest to the green marker or in the midblock.”
The observed discrepancies are likely due to differences in how the measure was applied, as we considered the crosswalk only at the starting point of the segment.
